elements.sent-2411-001: We study the problem of Joint Communication and Sensing for data transmission systems using optimal quantum instruments in order to transmit data and, at the same time, estimate environmental parameters.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular we consider the specific but at the same time generic case of a noiseless bosonic classical-quantum channel where part of the transmitted light is reflected back to the transmitter.
elements.sent-2411-003: While sending messages to the receiver, the transmitter tries at the same time to estimate the reflectivity of the channel.
elements.sent-2411-004: Extending earlier results on similar but finite-dimensional systems, we are able to characterize optimal tradeoffs between communication and detection rates.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also compare quantum performance to analogous classical models, quantifying the quantum advantage.
elements.sent-2411-006: The goal is to use existing networks to perform sensing in what are called perceptive mobile networks (PMN) \cite{jcassurvey2 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: This has motivated research of information-theoretic models that combine both tasks to adequately describe the tradeoff between them.
elements.sent-2411-008: Intuitively, it is expected that a system optimized for data transmission would be worse at sensing than one optimized for sensing (and vice versa), so that nontrivial tradeoffs would arise.
elements.sent-2411-009: For an introduction in the topic see \cite{jcasbook .}
elements.sent-2411-010: As quantum sensing \cite{Dege2017,Pirandola2018 and quantum communication \cite{Gisin2007 have become fruitful fields, it seems natural to extend \gls{jcas into the formalism of quantum mechanics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, the superadditivity of quantum capacity and the ability to perform collective measurements that outperform any local ones for state discrimination are a strong motivation for generalizing \gls{jcas to the quantum regime.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, \gls{jcas would benefit IOT by allowing each device to not only connect to others but also work as a sensor and then share information with other devices.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The intensity of reflections when operating in this manner is expected to be small, thus providing an interesting scenario to investigate quantum advantage, which is known to be larger in low photon regimes.
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite its potential advantages and interest, literature on the topic is scarce.
elements.sent-2411-015: A first result in the quantum aspects of transmitter-based \gls{jcas was studied in \cite{Wang_2022 for finite-dimensional systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Namely, it was shown how to quantify the performance of a sensing task for a classical-quantum (c-q) channel in open-loop coding (constant code, not adaptive based on measurement of previous measurements) and that in that scenario the performance of codewords only depends on the empirical distribution of symbols in the codewords.

elements.sent-2411-113: In this work we proposed a simple model for Quantum \gls{jcas , the \gls{blbc , that can be analitically solved using the current theoretical knowledge in quantum communication and quantum hypothesis testing.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In this channel there is no tradeoff for communication and detection, energy being the limiting factor for both tasks.
elements.sent-2411-115: We compared the performance of optimal quantum measurements to homodyne measurement as given in \cite{Hamdi2022 , finding a factor 4 improvement in detection rate while achieving the Holevo capacity, which is strictly larger than Shannon capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Moreover, the Holevo capacity has an unbounded advantage in low photon regimes.
elements.sent-2411-117: We note that the model presented in this paper only includes loss and doesn't consider other typical effects that happen in free space or optical fiber communication.
elements.sent-2411-118: Other interesting effects to include in the model would be thermal noise, fiber non-linearities and turbulence.
elements.sent-2411-119: We also assume a POVM at the receiver that achieves the Chernoff bound, which is not possible to implement with the current state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-120: Moreover, this POVM is not constructed directly and might be arbitrarily complex to implement.
elements.sent-2411-121: Extensive experimental research would be needed to develop detectors capable of implementing such a POVM.
elements.sent-2411-122: Further research should include gaussian channel models and gaussian input states in general, as both quantum communication in gaussian channels and discrimination of gaussian states are well-known topics \cite{Giovannetti_2014,gaussianHypothesisTesting, Weedbrook_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: Thus one expects these models to be analytically solvable while at the same time being practically interesting for tasks such as free space or optical fiber communication.
